Overall efficiency is IMPORTANT but not always the top priority [eval] — in commodity markets, efficiency is paramount; in differentiated markets (premium, service-led), customer experience and innovation matter more, and brutal efficiency drives can harm them. {BUSINESS} should pursue efficiency that doesn't damage what customers value — efficiency that erodes customer experience is false economy [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'research and development'.
model_answer: The activity of designing and creating new or improved products, services, or processes — typically involving scientists, engineers, designers, and significant investment.

example_question: Identify two industries with high R&D spending.
model_answer: Pharmaceuticals; semiconductors; biotechnology; aerospace; automotive (especially EVs); software/tech; defence; chemicals; consumer electronics; advanced materials.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} might invest in R&D.
model_answer: Develop new products that differentiate {BUSINESS} from competitors, supporting premium pricing and protecting market share in markets where products quickly become obsolete. Improve existing products to keep them competitive — cheaper to produce, better quality, more features — extending product life and customer satisfaction.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two risks of R&D investment for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Uncertain returns — many R&D projects fail to produce commercially successful products, so the money spent generates no revenue, and a string of failures can drain cash without producing any winning product. High upfront cost — R&D requires expensive scientists, engineers and facilities for years before any product emerges, locking up capital that could be earning returns elsewhere, with the payoff distant and uncertain.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should spend heavily on R&D or focus on improving current products? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Heavy R&D creates breakthrough products that command premium prices and disrupt markets — high-risk, high-reward strategy. Incremental improvement (kaizen) of current products is lower risk and protects existing margins. Overall the right strategy depends on the industry [eval] — fast-moving tech and pharma need heavy R&D to survive disruption; stable consumer products benefit more from improving what works. Most successful businesses do BOTH — incremental improvement for steady gains plus selective bigger R&D bets on the future. Pure incremental risks being out-innovated; pure R&D risks burning cash on failed bets [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'mass customisation'.
model_answer: Producing products that are tailored to individual customer preferences but still benefit from the cost efficiency of mass production — usually through modular design, configurable options, or digital production technologies.

example_question: Identify two examples of mass customisation.
model_answer: Configurable cars (choose colour, trim, engine); Nike personalised trainers; bespoke salads at fast-food chains; customised PCs (Dell); printed-on-demand books; personalised mugs/t-shirts; ear-fitted hearing aids; orthotic insoles; custom skincare formulas; smart-home configurations.

example_question: Outline two ways mass customisation benefits {BUSINESS} and customers.
model_answer: Customers get a product tailored to their specific needs and preferences, willing to pay a premium and feeling stronger emotional connection to the brand, raising loyalty. {BUSINESS} differentiates from mass-market competitors and captures higher margins through customisation, while still achieving scale economies on the modular components, getting some of the benefits of both job and flow production.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges {BUSINESS} faces in offering mass customisation.
model_answer: Operational complexity — managing thousands of product variants requires sophisticated systems for ordering, production planning, inventory and delivery, all far more complex than mass production. Mistakes are common and costly. Cost — customised products cost more to produce than fully-mass-produced ones, even with smart design. If customers won't pay enough premium, mass customisation loses money. Getting the price-cost balance right is hard.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think mass customisation is the future of manufacturing for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Mass customisation meets growing customer expectation of personalisation and uses modern technology (digital production, e-commerce) to deliver it economically. However, true mass markets for commodity items (basic food, fuel, household basics) still favour pure mass production, where customers prioritise price over personalisation. Overall mass customisation IS the future for many categories [eval] — fashion, technology, automotive, services — but not all. Commodity categories will continue to be served by traditional mass production. {BUSINESS} should assess where on the spectrum its products sit — categories where customers value personalisation and can pay for it should embrace mass customisation; pure commodity categories should not [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'logistics and distribution'.
model_answer: The process of moving goods from where they're produced to where they're consumed — including transport, warehousing, packaging, and inventory management.

example_question: Identify two methods of transporting goods.
model_answer: Road (lorries, vans); rail; sea (containers, bulk ships); air (cargo planes); pipeline (oil, gas); courier services (parcels); local delivery vehicles; intermodal (combination of methods); drones (emerging).

example_question: Outline two factors {BUSINESS} considers when choosing a transport method.
model_answer: Cost — air is fastest but most expensive; sea is cheapest but slowest. {BUSINESS} balances speed against cost for each product type. Nature of the product — perishable food needs fast (air) or refrigerated transport; bulk commodities suit sea/rail; valuable small items often go by courier with insurance.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways improving logistics could benefit {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Faster delivery to customers improves satisfaction and competitive position — modern customers expect quick delivery, and being faster than rivals wins orders. Amazon's success is largely a logistics achievement. Lower costs through route optimisation, load consolidation and warehouse efficiency directly reduce {BUSINESS}'s cost base, supporting better margins or lower prices.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think investing in logistics technology is the best way to improve customer satisfaction at {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Better logistics speeds delivery and reduces errors — for businesses where delivery is the customer touchpoint (online retail, food delivery, e-commerce), this is critical. However, customers also care about product quality, price and service. Logistics tech is expensive and may not be the highest-impact investment for businesses where customers interact mainly through products or store experience. Overall logistics IS critical for {BUSINESS} where delivery speed and reliability define the customer experience [eval] (online retail, food, urgent services). For businesses where product quality drives loyalty, logistics improvements support but don't replace excellence in the core product. The right investment depends on what customers value most for that specific business [eval]).
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'short-term sources of finance'.
model_answer: Money borrowed or raised for a short period (usually under one year) — used to manage day-to-day cash flow and short-term needs.

example_question: Identify two short-term sources of finance.
model_answer: Bank overdraft; trade credit (paying suppliers later); short-term bank loan; invoice factoring; debt factoring; credit card; payday lending; advances from customers; small business grants; supplier credit.

example_question: Outline two situations where {BUSINESS} might need short-term finance.
model_answer: Cover a temporary cash flow gap when revenue is delayed (e.g. waiting for big customer to pay) but bills must still be paid. Fund a one-off opportunity like buying discounted stock or jumping on a quick order, where finance is only needed until the resulting sales come in.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why short-term finance can be expensive for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Higher interest rates — short-term lending (overdrafts, credit cards) typically carries higher rates than long-term loans because lenders price in the higher risk and admin cost of brief lending, raising {BUSINESS}'s borrowing cost. Fees and charges — set-up fees, arrangement charges, late payment penalties add to the headline interest rate, so the total cost of short-term finance often exceeds what the rate alone suggests.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should rely on overdrafts for ongoing cash needs? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Overdrafts are flexible and only cost when used, useful for occasional gaps. However, ongoing reliance suggests deeper cash flow problems, and overdrafts can be withdrawn by the bank at short notice, creating a sudden crisis. Overall {BUSINESS} should NOT rely on overdrafts for ongoing needs [eval] — they're a tool for occasional emergencies and short-term cash gaps. Ongoing reliance indicates a structural problem that needs addressing (slow customer payment, weak pricing, excess inventory) rather than masked with short-term debt. {BUSINESS} should fix the underlying issue, not just keep borrowing [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'long-term sources of finance'.
model_answer: Money borrowed or raised for more than one year — used to fund major capital expenditure like buildings, machinery, business expansion, or acquisitions.

example_question: Identify two long-term sources of finance.
model_answer: Long-term bank loan; mortgage; share capital (selling shares); retained profit; debentures (corporate bonds); venture capital; private equity; leasing of equipment; government grants; crowdfunding.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} might use long-term rather than short-term finance.
model_answer: Matches the life of the investment — buying a 20-year factory should be funded with finance of similar duration, not short-term debt that needs constant refinancing. Lower interest rates than short-term debt for the same amount, making long-term commitment cheaper overall for big investments.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two factors {BUSINESS} should consider when choosing between a bank loan and a share issue.
model_answer: Cost vs control — loans require interest payments but keep all ownership with existing owners. Share issues require no interest but dilute ownership and may require future dividends, plus give shareholders rights to influence decisions. The right choice depends on whether {BUSINESS} values control or cash flow more. Risk profile — loans must be repaid on schedule regardless of profitability, threatening survival if revenue falls; share capital has no repayment obligation, so equity is safer in uncertain times but more expensive in good times.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should fund growth through debt or equity? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Debt is cheaper (interest is tax-deductible, no profit-sharing) and preserves control. Equity is less risky in tough times because there's no repayment pressure, and brings in shareholders who may add expertise. Overall the right mix depends on the project [eval] — for predictable, asset-backed investments with steady cash flow, debt usually wins on cost. For high-risk projects (R&D, new markets, untested products) equity is wiser because failure doesn't bankrupt {BUSINESS}. Most businesses use a mix proportionate to risk and lifecycle stage [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'internal vs external finance'.
model_answer: Internal: finance generated from within the business (retained profit, sale of assets, working capital). External: finance from outside (loans, share issues, grants, leasing).

example_question: Identify two sources of internal finance and two sources of external finance.
model_answer: Internal: retained profit; sale of assets; reduced working capital (less inventory, faster collections). External: bank loans; share issues; grants; venture capital; trade credit; crowdfunding; debentures; leasing.

example_question: Outline two advantages of internal finance for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: No interest payments — internal finance is essentially free, improving margins compared to debt-funded investment. No loss of control — using existing cash doesn't dilute ownership or give creditors influence, so owners retain full control of {BUSINESS}.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why {BUSINESS} might need external finance despite available internal options.
model_answer: Speed and scale — internal sources are limited; major opportunities (acquisition, new factory, big launch) may require more capital than {BUSINESS} has accumulated, so external finance enables seizing time-sensitive opportunities. Preserve cash buffer — using all retained profit for growth leaves {BUSINESS} vulnerable to cash crunches; external finance keeps reserves intact, providing a safety net for unexpected problems.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should always prefer internal finance to external finance? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Internal finance is cheaper, faster and preserves control — usually the first choice. However, relying only on internal sources limits growth pace, since retained profit builds slowly, and competitors using external finance can outpace organic growth. Overall {BUSINESS} should USE INTERNAL FIRST [eval] but not exclusively — internal for routine growth and reserves, external for big strategic moves where the opportunity is bigger than internal funds can support. Always preferring internal risks being left behind by competitors who borrow to seize moments [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'bank loan'.
model_answer: Money borrowed from a bank at a fixed or variable interest rate, repaid in regular instalments over an agreed term — usually secured against assets.

example_question: Identify two features of a typical bank loan.
model_answer: Fixed term (e.g. 5 years); regular monthly repayments; interest rate (fixed or variable); usually requires security/collateral; agreed amount; written agreement with conditions; sometimes early-repayment fees; credit check on borrower.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} might choose a bank loan over an overdraft.
model_answer: Lower interest rate per pound borrowed compared to overdrafts, reducing the cost of long-term capital expenditure. Predictable repayment schedule helps {BUSINESS} plan cash flow, while overdrafts have no fixed terms and can be withdrawn at short notice.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges {BUSINESS} faces when applying for a bank loan.
model_answer: Security requirements — banks usually require collateral (business assets, owner's home) to secure the loan, exposing personal or business assets to seizure if {BUSINESS} can't repay. This is a serious risk for owners. Credit assessment — banks demand detailed business plans, financial statements, forecasts and credit history. New businesses or those with weak finances may be rejected or get unfavourable terms, limiting access for those who most need finance.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think bank loans are the best source of finance for established small businesses? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Bank loans offer predictable, large-scale finance at lower rates than alternatives. However, they require collateral, are inflexible if cash flow fluctuates, and applications can be slow and rejected. Overall bank loans ARE usually the best source for established small businesses [eval] funding predictable assets (equipment, premises) — but for working capital needs, asset finance or invoice factoring may be better, and for high-risk growth, equity may suit better. The right finance source depends on the purpose [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'overdraft'.
model_answer: An arrangement with a bank allowing a business to spend more than its current balance, up to an agreed limit — flexible short-term borrowing for cash flow.

example_question: Identify two characteristics of a bank overdraft.
model_answer: Pre-agreed limit; interest charged only on amount used; flexible (use as needed); can be withdrawn by bank at short notice; usually variable interest rate; daily compound interest; often requires renewal annually; arrangement fee.

example_question: Outline two advantages of an overdraft over a bank loan.
model_answer: Pay interest only on what you use — if {BUSINESS} has overdraft facility of £50k but only uses £10k some months, it only pays interest on the £10k, far cheaper than a fixed loan of the same amount. Flexible — can be used when needed and repaid quickly, supporting irregular cash flow patterns rather than rigid scheduled repayments.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two risks of relying on overdrafts at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Bank can withdraw the facility at short notice — overdrafts are technically repayable on demand, so if the bank loses confidence, {BUSINESS} could face sudden full repayment, triggering a crisis with no time to find alternative finance. Higher interest rates than long-term loans for the same borrowing, so using overdraft for ongoing needs (rather than emergencies) costs significantly more than alternative financing.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should have a permanent overdraft facility in place? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Having facility in place provides instant access to cash for emergencies, even if rarely used — a useful safety net. However, banks charge arrangement fees and may withdraw the facility if {BUSINESS}'s circumstances change, providing false security. Overall {BUSINESS} SHOULD have facility for emergencies [eval] but should never USE it for ongoing operations. The facility is insurance — pay the small annual fee for peace of mind. But financial planning should aim to never actually need it, with retained reserves or committed credit lines for predictable needs [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'trade credit'.
model_answer: An arrangement where {BUSINESS} buys goods or services from suppliers but pays later — typically 30, 60, or 90 days after delivery, providing effectively interest-free short-term finance.

example_question: Identify two typical features of trade credit.
model_answer: Pay 30/60/90 days after invoice; usually interest-free; offered by suppliers to business customers; based on trust and credit history; some suppliers offer discount for early payment; reduces by amount of credit when supplier stops; not always agreed in advance; can be revoked if payments are late.

example_question: Outline two reasons why trade credit is attractive to {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Effectively interest-free short-term finance — {BUSINESS} gets goods now and pays later without paying interest, smoothing cash flow without expensive borrowing. Requires no formal application — just needs a good payment history with suppliers, much easier than getting bank approval.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two risks of using trade credit at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Damaging supplier relationships if {BUSINESS} pays late or unreliably — suppliers may withdraw credit terms, demand cash up front, or stop supplying entirely, disrupting operations. Hidden cost when discounts are missed — many suppliers offer 2-3% discount for early payment, so missing those discounts effectively costs {BUSINESS} an annualised rate equivalent to expensive borrowing, even though no 'interest' is charged.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should maximise its use of trade credit? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Maximum trade credit minimises cash use and effectively borrows interest-free. However, stretching payments damages relationships, misses early-payment discounts and risks losing trade credit altogether if suppliers tire of slow payment. Overall {BUSINESS} should use trade credit STRATEGICALLY [eval] — pay on time to maintain relationships and credit availability, but use the full period offered rather than paying early unless discounts justify it. Maximum stretching is short-sighted; balanced use is wise [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'share issue'.
model_answer: Raising finance by selling new shares in the business to investors — either to existing shareholders (rights issue), new investors (placing), or the general public (IPO/PLC flotation).

example_question: Identify two types of share issue.
model_answer: Initial public offering (IPO) — first time shares are sold to public; rights issue — existing shareholders offered new shares; placing — shares sold to selected institutional investors; private equity sale; employee share schemes.

example_question: Outline two advantages of a share issue for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Raises large sums of capital without taking on debt, so {BUSINESS} doesn't have repayment obligations or interest payments, supporting major investment. Permanent capital — once raised, share capital doesn't have to be repaid (unlike loans), strengthening the balance sheet long-term.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two disadvantages of issuing shares for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Loss of control — new shareholders gain voting rights and influence over major decisions, potentially altering {BUSINESS}'s direction in ways the original owners don't want. Dividend expectations — shareholders expect a return through dividends or rising share price, putting ongoing pressure on {BUSINESS} to deliver profits and distribute them, which may conflict with reinvestment plans.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think a share issue is a better way to fund expansion than borrowing? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Share issue avoids debt repayment pressure and brings in capital permanently, supporting riskier or longer-term expansion. However, it dilutes ownership, dilutes profits per share and gives outsiders influence. Overall the right choice depends on the project [eval] — for high-risk, uncertain-return investments, share issue spreads risk and avoids putting the business under debt-service pressure if things go wrong. For predictable, asset-backed investments with steady returns, debt is cheaper and preserves control. {BUSINESS} should match the financing type to the risk type [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'retained profit'.
model_answer: Profit after tax that the business keeps and reinvests rather than distributing to shareholders as dividends — the most common source of internal finance.

example_question: Identify two uses of retained profit at a business.
model_answer: Reinvestment in new equipment; expanding into new markets; funding research and development; building cash reserves; reducing existing debt; acquiring other businesses; opening new branches/sites; launching new products; investing in training and staff.

example_question: Outline two advantages of using retained profit to fund growth.
model_answer: No interest costs or repayment obligations, so {BUSINESS}'s margins and cash flow aren't strained, supporting healthier long-term operations. No dilution of ownership — using own profit doesn't bring in new shareholders, so existing owners retain control and full benefit of future success.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two limitations of relying on retained profit at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Limited amounts available — retained profit builds slowly, so {BUSINESS} may not have enough to fund major projects (new factory, acquisition) without years of saving, missing time-sensitive opportunities. Reduces dividends to shareholders, who may prefer current income to reinvestment — pushing too hard on retention can frustrate investors and depress share price.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should retain most of its profit for reinvestment, or distribute it as dividends? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Retention funds growth without borrowing and signals confidence in future returns. Dividends reward shareholders, attract income-focused investors and signal financial strength. Overall the right balance depends on growth opportunities [eval] — young, fast-growing businesses with high-return projects should retain most profit (Amazon famously paid no dividends for decades). Mature businesses with limited growth options should distribute more, since shareholders can invest the cash better elsewhere. {BUSINESS} should retain enough to fund profitable growth, return the rest [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'cash flow'.
model_answer: The movement of money in and out of a business over a period — inflows (sales, loans, investment) minus outflows (wages, suppliers, rent, repayments).

example_question: Identify two cash inflows and two cash outflows for a typical business.
model_answer: Inflows: sales revenue; loan from bank; investment from owners; sale of assets; interest received; grants received. Outflows: wages; supplier payments; rent; utilities; loan repayments; tax; dividends paid; new equipment.

example_question: Outline two reasons why positive cash flow is essential for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Pays day-to-day bills — wages, rent, suppliers must be paid even if profitable, and without cash {BUSINESS} can't operate regardless of long-term outlook. Survives unexpected events — strong cash flow lets {BUSINESS} weather downturns, supply disruptions or sudden costs without crisis or having to scramble for emergency finance.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why {BUSINESS} could be profitable but still run out of cash.
model_answer: Customers paying slowly — if {BUSINESS} sells on 60-day credit but pays suppliers in 30 days and pays staff weekly, profit on paper is locked in receivables, while cash leaves the business now. The faster the growth, the worse this becomes. Investing in inventory or assets — buying stock or new equipment turns cash into items, which produce future profit but reduce current cash. Profitable rapid growth often consumes more cash than it generates short-term.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think cash flow is more important than profit for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Cash flow keeps {BUSINESS} alive day-to-day — failing on cash is what closes businesses, even profitable ones. Profit is necessary long-term but you can't pay wages with future profit. Overall in the SHORT TERM cash is more important than profit [eval] — businesses fail from cash crises, not from low profits. In the LONG TERM both matter, with profit ultimately needed to generate cash. The two are linked but cash discipline saves businesses; pure profit focus without cash management kills them [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'cash flow forecast'.
model_answer: A prediction of future cash inflows and outflows over a period (usually weekly or monthly for 6-12 months ahead), showing expected cash balance at each point.

example_question: Identify two components of a cash flow forecast.
model_answer: Opening balance; cash inflows (sales, loans, investment); cash outflows (wages, supplier payments, rent, tax); net cash flow (inflows minus outflows); closing balance; identification of cash gaps; assumptions and risks.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} should prepare a cash flow forecast.
model_answer: Spot future cash shortages in advance so {BUSINESS} can arrange finance (overdraft, loan, customer payments) before the crisis hits, rather than scrambling under pressure. Banks and investors expect forecasts as part of any finance application, so without one, {BUSINESS} can't access external funding.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why cash flow forecasts often turn out wrong for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Sales forecasts are usually too optimistic — owners overestimate how much customers will buy and how quickly they'll pay, so cash inflows arrive later or smaller than planned. Unexpected events — one major customer paying late, equipment breakdown, supplier price rise, can all upset the forecast within days. No model can predict every shock.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should rely on its cash flow forecast for important decisions? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Forecasts give early warning of cash problems and support planning — far better than no projection. However, forecasts are estimates not certainties, and businesses that treat them as guarantees miss the surprises that always happen. Overall {BUSINESS} should USE forecasts AS A GUIDE [eval] not a guarantee — update monthly with actual data, run different scenarios (best, worst, likely) and build in contingency rather than depending on the central forecast. Forecasts identify risks; cash buffers handle the surprises [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'cash flow problems'.
model_answer: Situations where a business cannot pay its bills as they fall due — even if profitable on paper, lack of cash can cause major problems including insolvency.

example_question: Identify two common causes of cash flow problems for businesses.
model_answer: Customers paying late; over-investment in inventory; overtrading (growing too fast); seasonal sales dips; major customer loss; unexpected costs (equipment failure); economic downturn; tax demand; poor credit control; investing in assets.

example_question: Outline two short-term ways {BUSINESS} could resolve a cash flow crisis.
model_answer: Arrange an overdraft or short-term loan to bridge the gap, providing immediate cash to pay urgent bills. Speed up customer payments by offering early-payment discounts, chasing overdue invoices, or factoring receivables to a finance company.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two long-term solutions to cash flow problems at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Improve credit control — shorter payment terms, more rigorous chasing of late payers, and refusing to supply persistent slow-payers ensures cash comes in faster, reducing the gap between sale and cash receipt. Build a cash buffer — retain profits as cash reserves rather than spending or distributing all profit, so {BUSINESS} has resilience to weather future cash flow stress without external borrowing.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think most cash flow problems result from external causes or internal mismanagement? Justify your answer.
model_answer: External shocks (recession, customer bankruptcy, sudden costs) clearly cause real cash flow crises that no business can entirely prevent. However, well-managed businesses with strong reserves, good credit control and prudent growth typically survive shocks that destroy poorly-managed ones. Overall most cash flow problems are CAUSED INTERNALLY [eval] even when triggered externally — weak credit control, over-trading, insufficient reserves and over-investment in assets create the vulnerability. External events are the trigger but internal weaknesses are the underlying cause. The same external event affects different businesses differently based on their internal practices [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'break-even analysis'.
model_answer: Calculation showing the level of sales (in units or revenue) at which a business covers all its costs and starts to make profit — break-even point = fixed costs ÷ contribution per unit.

example_question: Identify two key figures used in break-even analysis.
model_answer: Fixed costs (don't change with output); variable costs per unit; selling price per unit; contribution per unit (price minus variable cost); break-even point (units); break-even revenue (£); margin of safety.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} would calculate its break-even point.
model_answer: Sets a minimum sales target — knowing exactly how many units must be sold to cover costs gives a clear goal for sales and marketing teams. Tests viability before launch — if break-even seems unachievable given realistic market size, {BUSINESS} can adjust price, costs or scrap the plan before incurring losses.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two limitations of break-even analysis at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Assumes everything else is constant — real markets change (prices vary, fixed costs creep up, variable costs fluctuate), so the calculated break-even point becomes inaccurate over time. Ignores demand — break-even tells {BUSINESS} how much it MUST sell, not how much it CAN sell. The breakeven point might be achievable in theory but unattainable in practice given market size.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think break-even analysis is useful enough to justify the effort for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Break-even is simple to calculate and gives clear minimum sales targets, valuable for planning and pricing decisions. However, the assumptions are restrictive (linear costs, single price, all units sold) and real businesses rarely match. Overall break-even IS useful for {BUSINESS} [eval] as a quick reality check on viability, especially for new products or businesses. But it shouldn't be the only analysis — combine with market research on likely demand, sensitivity analysis (what if costs rise 10%?), and cash flow forecasting for a fuller picture. Used as one tool among several, break-even is genuinely valuable [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'margin of safety'.
model_answer: The difference between actual or expected sales and the break-even point — usually expressed as a percentage or units. A larger margin of safety means more buffer against falling sales.

example_question: Identify two ways margin of safety can be measured.
model_answer: In units (actual sales units minus break-even units); in revenue (£) terms; as a percentage of actual sales; as months of trading; as % above break-even.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} wants a large margin of safety.
model_answer: Provides cushion against falling demand — if sales drop by 20%, a small margin of safety means break-even is missed and losses start; a large margin absorbs the shock without immediate crisis. Indicates underlying business health — large margin means {BUSINESS} is far from break-even, signalling robust profitability to investors and lenders.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could increase its margin of safety.
model_answer: Lower the break-even point — reduce fixed costs (renegotiate rent, sublet space) or raise contribution per unit (better prices or lower variable costs), so fewer sales are needed to cover costs, widening the margin. Grow sales without growing fixed costs proportionately — more revenue from existing capacity directly increases units above break-even, widening the margin.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think a margin of safety of 10% is high enough for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: 10% margin means {BUSINESS} can lose 10% of sales before hitting losses — covers minor demand fluctuations or seasonal dips. However, 10% is thin for any significant shock — recessions, lost customers or supply disruptions easily cause bigger sales falls. Overall 10% is TOO TIGHT for most businesses [eval] — comfortable margin is usually 25-40% for healthy resilience. 10% suggests {BUSINESS} is operating close to the edge with limited room for error. The right target depends on volatility of the industry — stable utilities might survive 10%; volatile retail or hospitality needs much more [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'profit'.
model_answer: What remains from revenue after all costs have been deducted — gross profit (revenue minus cost of goods sold), operating profit, and net profit (after all expenses, interest, and tax) are different levels.

example_question: Identify the difference between gross profit and net profit.
model_answer: Gross profit = revenue minus cost of goods sold (direct costs of producing the products). Net profit = gross profit minus all other expenses including overheads, salaries, marketing, rent, interest and tax — what's left after every cost is paid.

example_question: Outline two reasons why profit is important to {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Provides return to owners or shareholders — without profit, there's no reward for the risk of investing, so investors won't fund the business and owners won't continue. Funds growth and reinvestment — profitable businesses can finance expansion from retained earnings without borrowing, building long-term sustainability and resilience.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could increase profit.
model_answer: Raise prices on products where customers are willing to pay more — if demand is inelastic, modest price rises generate more revenue at little volume loss, boosting profit. Reduce costs without affecting customer value — negotiate better supplier deals, eliminate waste, automate routine tasks; these directly improve margins without losing customers.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think maximising profit should always be the top objective for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Profit funds survival, growth and rewards investors — essential and unavoidable. However, single-minded profit maximisation can damage customer relationships, employee morale, environmental impact and long-term sustainability. Overall pure maximisation is NOT always right [eval] — stakeholder businesses balancing profit with employee wellbeing, customer satisfaction and social responsibility often outperform pure profit-maximisers in the long run. {BUSINESS} needs profit to survive but treating it as the ONLY goal creates short-termism that destroys the conditions for future profit. Sustainable profit, not maximum profit, is the right target [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'profit margin'.
model_answer: Profit expressed as a percentage of sales revenue — shows what proportion of each pound of sales becomes profit. Gross margin and net margin are different ratios.

example_question: Identify the formulas for gross profit margin and net profit margin.
model_answer: Gross profit margin = (Gross profit ÷ Revenue) × 100. Net profit margin = (Net profit ÷ Revenue) × 100.

example_question: Outline two reasons why profit margin matters more than total profit amount.
model_answer: Allows comparison between businesses of different sizes — a £1m profit on £10m revenue (10% margin) is healthier than £1m on £20m revenue (5% margin). Identifies efficiency — high margins indicate strong cost control and pricing power; low margins signal vulnerability to competition and cost pressures, even when absolute profit looks acceptable.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could improve its profit margin.
model_answer: Increase prices on products with strong demand or unique features — if customers won't switch, every penny of price increase goes straight to margin, dramatically improving profitability without volume effort. Cut costs without affecting quality — negotiate supplier deals, reduce waste, automate routine work, all reduce cost per sale while leaving revenue intact, raising margin.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think low margins always indicate that {BUSINESS} is in trouble? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Low margins do indicate vulnerability — small cost rises or price pressure quickly turn profit into loss, and low-margin businesses must work harder to make decent absolute profit. However, some industries (supermarkets, airlines, distributors) operate on naturally low margins because of scale or competition; high volume compensates for thin margin. Overall low margins are NOT always a problem [eval] — depends on industry norms and trend. Margins below sector norms are a warning; margins consistent with the industry but stable are acceptable. Trend matters more than absolute level — declining margins always signal trouble; stable low margins may be fine [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'income statement'.
model_answer: A financial statement showing a business's revenues, costs, and profits over a period (usually a year or quarter) — also called profit and loss account.

example_question: Identify two items typically shown on an income statement.
model_answer: Revenue (sales); cost of goods sold; gross profit; operating expenses (rent, wages, utilities); operating profit; interest paid; tax; net profit; depreciation; dividends paid.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} prepares an income statement.
model_answer: Legal requirement — limited companies must publish income statements for shareholders, tax authorities and Companies House. Shows performance — owners and managers track revenue growth, cost trends and profitability over time, supporting decisions about pricing, costs and investment.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways managers at {BUSINESS} could use information from the income statement.
model_answer: Compare actual vs budget — variances reveal whether sales targets are being met, costs are under control and profitability matches expectations, prompting corrective action where needed. Identify cost categories that are growing too fast — analysing each cost line over time shows where costs are escalating (e.g. marketing, salaries, utilities), letting management investigate causes and intervene before profits suffer significantly.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think the income statement is the most important financial document for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Income statement shows the bottom line — whether the business made money over the period — clearly important and what most stakeholders examine first. However, it doesn't show cash position, balance sheet strength or future outlook, all critical to true financial health. Overall the income statement is IMPORTANT but not most important alone [eval] — combined with balance sheet (financial position) and cash flow statement (cash movements), it gives a complete picture. Looking at any single statement misses key insights. Stakeholders should always review all three together [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'balance sheet'.
model_answer: A financial statement showing a business's assets (what it owns), liabilities (what it owes), and equity (owners' stake) at a specific point in time — assets = liabilities + equity.

example_question: Identify two types of asset and two types of liability typically shown on a balance sheet.
model_answer: Assets: cash; inventory; receivables (money owed by customers); buildings; equipment; vehicles; investments; intellectual property. Liabilities: trade payables (owed to suppliers); bank loans; tax owed; accrued expenses; long-term debt; lease obligations; bonds.

example_question: Outline two reasons why a balance sheet is important to lenders and investors.
model_answer: Shows financial position — total assets, total liabilities and net worth at a point in time, revealing whether the business has enough resources and isn't over-indebted. Calculates key ratios — liquidity (current ratio), gearing (debt-to-equity), asset utilisation, all critical for assessing whether {BUSINESS} is a safe place to lend or invest.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two pieces of information {BUSINESS} can learn from analysing its balance sheet over time.
model_answer: Working capital trend — current assets minus current liabilities shows whether short-term financial position is improving or deteriorating, signalling whether {BUSINESS} has enough liquid resources to operate smoothly. Capital structure — ratio of debt to equity shows whether the business is becoming over-leveraged, signalling rising financial risk if debt grows faster than profits or assets.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think the balance sheet alone gives a true picture of {BUSINESS}'s health? Justify your answer.
model_answer: The balance sheet shows financial position at a point in time, including key indicators like solvency and liquidity. However, it's static (just one moment), doesn't show profitability or cash flow over time, and asset values may not reflect current market reality. Overall the balance sheet alone is NOT enough [eval] — it complements but doesn't replace the income statement (profitability) and cash flow statement (movement). True financial health requires all three together, plus context about industry, strategy and market position. Looking at balance sheet in isolation misses the full picture [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'working capital'.
model_answer: The capital used in day-to-day operations — calculated as current assets (cash, inventory, receivables) minus current liabilities (payables, short-term debt). Positive working capital means short-term assets exceed short-term debts.

example_question: Identify two components of working capital.
model_answer: Cash; inventory (stock); trade receivables (customers owe); short-term investments; trade payables (owed to suppliers); short-term loans; tax due within a year; accrued expenses.

example_question: Outline two reasons why managing working capital is important for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Ensures the business can pay short-term bills (wages, suppliers, rent) as they fall due, avoiding crisis or insolvency. Frees up cash for growth — minimising tied-up working capital (slow-paying customers, excess inventory) releases cash for investment without external borrowing.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could improve its working capital position.
model_answer: Speed up customer payments — shorter credit terms, early-payment discounts, stricter chasing convert receivables to cash faster, reducing capital tied up in unpaid invoices. Reduce inventory holdings — better forecasting and just-in-time stock management minimise inventory levels, freeing the cash currently sitting in warehouses without affecting customer service.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should try to minimise its working capital? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Minimising working capital frees cash for other uses and improves return on capital employed. However, too little working capital leaves no buffer for fluctuations and risks crises. Overall {BUSINESS} should OPTIMISE not minimise working capital [eval] — minimum needed to operate smoothly, plus reasonable buffer for shocks, but no excess. The goal is efficiency, not extremes. Industry-leading companies often have tight but not minimal working capital, balancing efficiency with resilience [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'liquidity'.
model_answer: A business's ability to pay its short-term debts as they fall due — measured by current ratio (current assets ÷ current liabilities) and quick ratio (current assets minus inventory ÷ current liabilities).

example_question: Identify two measures of liquidity.
model_answer: Current ratio (current assets ÷ current liabilities); quick ratio / acid test (current assets minus inventory ÷ current liabilities); cash ratio; working capital amount; days payable / receivable / inventory.

example_question: Outline two reasons why liquidity matters for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Pays day-to-day bills — without liquid assets {BUSINESS} can't pay wages, suppliers or rent on time, causing immediate operational problems regardless of long-term profitability. Reassures suppliers and lenders — strong liquidity signals financial health, supporting better trade credit terms, lower borrowing costs and continued relationships.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why a profitable business might still have liquidity problems.
model_answer: Cash tied up in inventory or receivables — sales generate profit on paper but the cash hasn't arrived yet, while suppliers, wages and tax must be paid now. Rapid growth especially creates this cash squeeze. Heavy investment in fixed assets — buying buildings or equipment requires major cash outflow, which doesn't appear as a cost on the income statement but reduces liquidity dramatically.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think liquidity is more important than profitability for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Liquidity keeps {BUSINESS} alive day-to-day; without it, even profitable businesses collapse. Profitability is essential for long-term survival but not enough on its own to ensure short-term survival. Overall LIQUIDITY MATTERS MORE in the short term, PROFITABILITY MORE in the long term [eval]. Both are essential but at different timescales. {BUSINESS} can survive periods of low profit if liquidity is strong; cannot survive a liquidity crisis even with strong profit. Smart management monitors both, with liquidity protecting today and profitability ensuring tomorrow [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'profitability ratios'.
model_answer: Ratios that measure how well a business converts sales into profit — including gross profit margin, net profit margin, and return on capital employed (ROCE).

example_question: Identify three profitability ratios used to analyse business performance.
model_answer: Gross profit margin (gross profit ÷ revenue × 100); net profit margin (net profit ÷ revenue × 100); return on capital employed / ROCE (operating profit ÷ capital employed × 100); operating profit margin; gross profit return on assets.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} should monitor its profitability ratios.
model_answer: Benchmark against competitors and industry averages to assess relative efficiency and identify where {BUSINESS} is winning or losing competitively. Track over time — falling ratios warn of cost pressures, pricing problems or weakening competitive position, often before absolute profit shows the trend.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two limitations of using profitability ratios alone to judge {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Ratios can be manipulated through accounting choices (different stock valuation methods, capitalised vs expensed costs), so two businesses with identical real performance can show different ratios depending on accounting policy. Ignores absolute scale — a small business with 30% margin earning £100k profit is less significant than a large business with 10% margin earning £100m. Ratios miss this context.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think gross profit margin or net profit margin is more useful to {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Gross margin reflects direct cost efficiency and pricing power — the fundamental health of the products. Net margin reflects total business efficiency including overheads, marketing and other costs. Overall BOTH MATTER for different decisions [eval] — gross for product-level and pricing analysis, net for overall management efficiency. Looking at gross without net misses overhead bloat; looking at net without gross hides whether product economics are sound. {BUSINESS} should use both in combination, drilling from net to gross to identify exactly where issues lie [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'return on capital employed'.
model_answer: ROCE measures how efficiently a business uses its capital to generate operating profit — Operating profit ÷ (Total assets - Current liabilities) × 100. Higher ROCE means better use of investor capital.

example_question: Identify the formula for ROCE.
model_answer: ROCE = (Operating profit ÷ Capital employed) × 100, where capital employed = Total assets minus current liabilities, OR shareholders' equity plus non-current liabilities.

example_question: Outline two reasons why ROCE is an important measure for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Shows how efficiently invested capital generates profit — high ROCE means {BUSINESS} is creating more value per pound invested than competitors. Comparable across businesses of different sizes, unlike absolute profit, supporting investor benchmarking.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could improve its ROCE.
model_answer: Increase operating profit through higher margins (better prices, lower costs) or higher sales volume without proportionate capital growth, raising the numerator without growing the denominator. Reduce capital employed by selling underused assets, leasing rather than buying equipment, or improving working capital efficiency, shrinking the denominator while maintaining profit.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think ROCE is the best single measure of {BUSINESS}'s financial performance? Justify your answer.
model_answer: ROCE captures both profitability and efficient use of capital in one number, very useful for investors comparing alternatives. However, it can be improved by reducing investment (which might hurt long-term competitiveness) and ignores cash flow, growth and risk. Overall ROCE is ONE OF THE BEST single measures [eval] but no single metric is enough — sustainable high ROCE matters more than absolute level (a temporary spike from cutting investment isn't real improvement). Best used alongside revenue growth, margins, cash flow and customer measures for a balanced view [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'budgeting'.
model_answer: Preparing detailed plans for expected income and expenditure over a period — used to control spending, set targets, monitor performance against plan, and allocate resources.

example_question: Identify two types of budget a business might prepare.
model_answer: Sales budget; production budget; cash budget; capital expenditure budget; marketing budget; salary budget; overhead budget; departmental budgets; flexible budget; master budget combining all others.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} prepares budgets.
model_answer: Coordinates departments — sales, production, finance and HR all need to plan around the same expected level of activity, so budgets force consistent assumptions across the business. Controls spending — managers know their budget limits and must justify exceeding them, preventing unplanned cost overruns and supporting financial discipline.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why budgets can sometimes harm performance at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Encourage end-of-year spending — departments that haven't used their budget rush to spend it before the period ends to avoid being cut next year, wasting money on unnecessary purchases just to 'use up' the allocation. Discourage initiative — strict budget limits prevent managers seizing unexpected opportunities or responding to threats outside the plan, slowing the business in fast-changing markets where flexibility matters.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think traditional annual budgets are still useful for fast-changing businesses? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Annual budgets provide planning structure, coordination, accountability — essential for any reasonable size business. However, in fast-changing industries, a budget set in December can be obsolete by March, requiring constant revision or being ignored. Overall traditional annual budgets are STILL USEFUL [eval] but need modernising — rolling forecasts updated quarterly, more flexible reallocation, and 'driver-based' budgets that adjust automatically with sales changes. The principles (coordination, accountability) are timeless; the rigid implementation needs updating for modern speed [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'fixed and variable costs'.
model_answer: Fixed costs don't change with output (rent, salaries, insurance). Variable costs change directly with output (raw materials, packaging, sales commission). Total cost = fixed + variable.

example_question: Identify two fixed costs and two variable costs for a typical business.
model_answer: Fixed: rent; salaries; insurance; depreciation of equipment; business rates; subscription software. Variable: raw materials; packaging; sales commission; hourly wage labour; energy used in production; delivery costs per order.

example_question: Outline two reasons why distinguishing fixed from variable costs matters for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Calculates break-even — fixed costs must be covered before profit starts, while variable costs scale with each sale. Knowing the split lets {BUSINESS} calculate exactly how many sales are needed. Supports pricing decisions — variable cost is the floor below which {BUSINESS} loses money on each sale; fixed costs determine the volume needed to cover overhead. Both matter for setting profitable prices.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two strategic implications of {BUSINESS}'s mix of fixed and variable costs.
model_answer: High fixed costs create operational gearing — once break-even is passed, every extra sale brings high marginal profit, but if sales fall, losses mount fast. High-fixed businesses are riskier but more profitable at scale. Variable cost dominance means lower risk — costs fall with sales, so even big drops in revenue don't trigger major losses, but the business doesn't benefit as much from scale either.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should aim to reduce its fixed costs and rely more on variable costs? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Lower fixed costs reduce break-even and operational risk, making {BUSINESS} more resilient in downturns. However, variable costs are typically higher per unit than equivalent fixed-cost solutions (renting versus owning, contractor vs employee), so reducing fixed costs by switching to variable usually means higher cost per unit at high volumes. Overall the right balance depends on volume stability [eval] — businesses with volatile demand benefit from variable cost structures; businesses with stable demand benefit from fixed costs that scale efficiently. The right structure matches the business's risk profile, not a blanket preference [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'crowdfunding'.
model_answer: Raising small amounts of money from a large number of people, typically online — for product launches, charitable causes, creative projects, or new ventures.

example_question: Identify two types of crowdfunding.
model_answer: Reward-based (Kickstarter, Indiegogo); equity crowdfunding (Crowdcube, Seedrs); donation-based (GoFundMe); peer-to-peer lending; debt crowdfunding; charity crowdfunding; subscription/patronage (Patreon).

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} might use crowdfunding.
model_answer: Tests market demand before launch — if many people pledge money for a product, it proves customer interest before {BUSINESS} commits to large production runs. Builds early customer community — backers become advocates and customers, providing initial sales and word-of-mouth marketing that's hard to buy.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two risks of crowdfunding for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Public failure — if the campaign doesn't reach its target, the failure is publicly visible and may damage {BUSINESS}'s credibility with future investors or customers. Delivery pressure — backers expect rewards or products by promised dates; delivery problems generate public criticism and complaints that hurt reputation, sometimes more than the financial benefit of crowdfunding was worth.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think crowdfunding is a viable alternative to traditional bank loans for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Crowdfunding raises capital without giving control to a bank, provides marketing and validation, and works for businesses banks won't fund (creative, consumer products, early stage). However, it suits only certain types of business (consumer-facing, exciting story, visual appeal) and platforms take fees. Overall crowdfunding is VIABLE for the right businesses [eval] — consumer products with photogenic appeal, creative projects, mission-driven ventures. For B2B services, industrial products or boring-but-essential businesses, banks and other traditional sources usually work better. The right finance route depends on business type, not just preference [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'investment appraisal'.
model_answer: Techniques for evaluating whether a proposed investment is worthwhile — including payback period, accounting rate of return (ARR), and net present value (NPV).

example_question: Identify two methods of investment appraisal.
model_answer: Payback period; accounting rate of return (ARR); net present value (NPV); internal rate of return (IRR); discounted payback; profitability index; cost-benefit analysis.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} should evaluate investments before committing.
model_answer: Avoids wasting capital on projects that won't earn back their cost — formal evaluation catches obvious losers before money is spent. Enables comparison between competing projects — when {BUSINESS} can only fund one of several options, appraisal techniques identify which generates the best return.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two limitations of using payback period alone to evaluate investments at {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Ignores returns after payback — payback might be 3 years, but one project might earn modest returns for 2 more years while another earns big returns for 10 more, yet payback treats them equally. Misses the long-term winner. Ignores the time value of money — £1000 received in year 5 is worth less than £1000 today (inflation, opportunity cost), but payback treats all years equally, biasing toward short-term projects.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think payback period is a good method for {BUSINESS} to use for investment decisions? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Payback is simple, intuitive and emphasises liquidity recovery — useful for risk-averse businesses where getting cash back quickly matters more than maximum return. However, it ignores returns beyond payback and time value of money, so it can lead to choosing inferior long-term projects. Overall payback is USEFUL as a first filter [eval] but should NOT be the only criterion. {BUSINESS} should use payback to screen out projects with intolerable risk, then use NPV or IRR to compare survivors. Different methods suit different decisions — relying on one is a mistake [eval]).
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'globalisation'.
model_answer: The increasing interconnection of countries through trade, investment, technology, and movement of people — making the world's economies more integrated.

example_question: Identify two features of globalisation.
model_answer: Increased international trade; growth of multinational companies; faster movement of money/capital across borders; spread of technology and ideas worldwide; increased migration; global supply chains; trade agreements (WTO, NAFTA); cultural exchange.

example_question: Outline two ways globalisation creates opportunities for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Access to larger markets — selling to customers globally multiplies the potential customer base, especially for businesses with unique or specialist products. Cheaper inputs — sourcing materials and components from low-cost countries reduces {BUSINESS}'s production costs and supports competitive pricing or better margins.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two threats globalisation poses to {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: International competition — global firms with lower costs (cheaper labour, scale economies) can enter {BUSINESS}'s home market and undercut on price, eroding market share. Supply chain dependency — relying on overseas suppliers exposes {BUSINESS} to disruption (transport, geopolitics, tariffs, pandemics) that local supply chains wouldn't face, creating new operational risks.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think globalisation has been good or bad for businesses like {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Globalisation opens markets and lowers input costs, helping competitive businesses access more customers and cheaper supplies. However, it also exposes them to fierce international competition that domestic-only businesses don't face. Overall globalisation is BENEFICIAL for {BUSINESS} that can compete internationally [eval] — accessing larger markets and cheaper inputs more than offsets increased competition. For purely domestic businesses, especially small ones, globalisation can be a threat unless they use global supply chains or focus on local strengths. The net impact depends on adaptation [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'multinational companies'.
model_answer: Businesses with operations (production, sales, services) in more than one country — typically owned and managed from a home-country headquarters.

example_question: Identify two well-known multinational companies.
model_answer: Apple; Coca-Cola; Toyota; Nestlé; Samsung; Microsoft; Amazon; McDonald's; Unilever; HSBC; Shell; BP; Volkswagen; Disney; Procter & Gamble.

example_question: Outline two reasons why a business becomes a multinational.
model_answer: Access new markets directly — by producing or selling in target countries, {BUSINESS} avoids export tariffs, adapts to local tastes, and competes on equal footing with local firms. Lower production costs — locating manufacturing in countries with cheaper labour or materials reduces unit costs, supporting competitive pricing globally.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges multinationals face.
model_answer: Cultural differences — what works in one country may not in another; products, marketing, management styles must be adapted, requiring local expertise that's expensive to develop. Political and currency risk — operating across many countries means exposure to political changes (regulation, expropriation, tariffs) and currency fluctuations that can suddenly damage profitability in ways that home-country-only businesses wouldn't face.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think the benefits of becoming a multinational outweigh the risks for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Going multinational brings scale, diversification and cost advantages — vital for ambitious businesses in mature home markets. However, complexity, costs and risks (cultural, political, currency) are substantial, and many multinational expansions destroy more value than they create. Overall going multinational IS beneficial if {BUSINESS} has the capacity and capital to handle complexity [eval] — for small or unprepared firms it can be a costly mistake. The decision depends on capability as much as opportunity — many firms over-extend themselves internationally before they're ready [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'exchange rate appreciation'.
model_answer: An increase in the value of a country's currency relative to others — the same amount of domestic currency buys more foreign currency.

example_question: Identify two effects of currency appreciation on a country's economy.
model_answer: Imports become cheaper; exports become more expensive; foreign tourism becomes more expensive (inbound declines); outbound travel becomes cheaper; domestic businesses lose competitiveness in export markets; consumer purchasing power rises; inflation may fall.

example_question: Outline two effects of currency appreciation on {BUSINESS} if it exports.
model_answer: Exports become more expensive in foreign markets, so demand for {BUSINESS}'s products may fall as overseas customers find cheaper alternatives. Imported raw materials become cheaper, reducing {BUSINESS}'s production costs (partial offset if {BUSINESS} also imports inputs).
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could respond to a sustained appreciation of its currency.
model_answer: Cut prices in foreign markets — accept lower margins to maintain volume, hoping the currency move reverses; risky if margin pressure is sustained. Move production to weaker-currency countries — locate manufacturing where costs are now relatively lower, restoring competitiveness in foreign markets. Long-term solution but costly to implement.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think currency appreciation is always bad for a country's businesses? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Currency appreciation hurts exporters and tourism but benefits importers, businesses needing foreign inputs, and consumers buying foreign goods. Overall the effect on a country's businesses is MIXED [eval] not uniformly bad. Export-dependent businesses suffer; import-dependent businesses benefit. Businesses with strong domestic markets and limited foreign exposure are unaffected. The aggregate impact depends on the country's economic structure. For most diversified economies, appreciation hurts some sectors and helps others, with the net effect modest. The simple 'appreciation = bad' view misses these nuances [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'exchange rate depreciation'.
model_answer: A decrease in the value of a country's currency relative to others — the same amount of domestic currency buys less foreign currency.

example_question: Identify two effects of currency depreciation on a country's economy.
model_answer: Exports become cheaper (more competitive); imports become more expensive; tourism into the country becomes cheaper (boost); travel abroad becomes more expensive; inflation may rise (imported costs); domestic businesses gain competitive advantage; balance of trade may improve.

example_question: Outline two effects of currency depreciation on {BUSINESS} if it imports raw materials.
model_answer: Imported raw materials become more expensive in domestic currency, raising production costs and squeezing margins unless {BUSINESS} can pass costs on. Forces {BUSINESS} to either accept lower profits, raise prices (risking sales), or find local substitutes (which may be lower quality or unavailable).
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could benefit from a weaker home currency.
model_answer: Increased export sales — products become cheaper in foreign markets, increasing overseas demand. Existing customers may buy more, new customers become accessible, supporting revenue growth. Tourism boost (if relevant) — international visitors find {BUSINESS}'s country more affordable, increasing demand for hospitality, retail and leisure businesses, particularly in tourist-heavy regions.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think a weak currency is always good for an exporting business? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Weak currency makes exports more competitive and grows foreign sales — clearly beneficial for export-led businesses. However, it raises import costs (often eroding margin), causes inflation (raising domestic costs), and may signal economic weakness that hurts overall demand. Overall a weak currency is GENERALLY POSITIVE for export-focused {BUSINESS} [eval] but not without complications — long-term economic weakness damages domestic conditions, and competitor countries may match the devaluation, removing the advantage. Best when temporary; problematic if persistent. {BUSINESS} should hedge currency risk and not rely on permanent favourable rates [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'import tariffs'.
model_answer: Taxes imposed on goods imported into a country — make foreign products more expensive and protect domestic producers, but also raise prices for consumers.

example_question: Identify two reasons a government might impose import tariffs.
model_answer: Protect domestic industries from foreign competition; raise government revenue; respond to unfair trade practices (anti-dumping); support new ('infant') industries; reduce trade deficit; protect national security industries; respond to political tensions.

example_question: Outline two effects of import tariffs on {BUSINESS} that imports.
model_answer: Higher costs of imported inputs make {BUSINESS}'s production more expensive, squeezing margins or forcing price rises that lose customers. May force switching to higher-cost domestic suppliers, raising production costs even more, or losing access to specialised imported components.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two effects on {BUSINESS} if its country imposes tariffs on its exports' destination.
model_answer: Loss of competitiveness abroad — tariffs make {BUSINESS}'s exports more expensive than local rivals' products, so foreign demand falls and {BUSINESS} loses sales in those markets. Retaliation risk — other countries often respond with their own tariffs, hurting {BUSINESS}'s ability to source materials internationally and creating broader trade friction that affects all aspects of operations.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think tariffs help or harm businesses like {BUSINESS} overall? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Tariffs protect specific domestic industries from foreign competition and can save jobs in those sectors. However, they raise input costs for businesses that import, hurt exporters facing retaliation, and reduce consumer purchasing power overall. Overall tariffs HURT MOST BUSINESSES [eval] even if they help some — most businesses interact with international trade in multiple ways (imports, exports, customers' costs), and tariffs disrupt all of these. The net effect on the economy is usually negative even when specific industries benefit. {BUSINESS} should favour free trade unless it's in a directly-protected industry [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.
