example_question: Define 'import quotas'.
model_answer: Limits on the quantity (volume) of a specific product that can be imported into a country in a given period — restricts foreign supply to protect domestic producers.

example_question: Identify two differences between tariffs and quotas.
model_answer: Tariffs are taxes on imports (raising price); quotas are physical limits on quantity. Tariffs generate government revenue; quotas typically don't (unless auctioned). Tariffs let imports continue at higher prices; quotas cap imports regardless of price.

example_question: Outline two effects of import quotas on {BUSINESS} as a domestic producer.
model_answer: Less foreign competition because total imports are capped, protecting {BUSINESS}'s market share regardless of price advantages foreign rivals might have. Higher prices may be possible domestically because foreign supply is restricted, supporting better margins.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two negative effects of import quotas on {BUSINESS} if it relies on imported components.
model_answer: Supply restriction — once the quota is filled, no more of that component can be imported, so {BUSINESS} may face shortages and production stoppages, losing revenue. Higher prices for available imports — limited supply within the quota drives up prices for imported components, raising {BUSINESS}'s costs and squeezing margins.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think import quotas are more effective than tariffs for protecting domestic industry? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Quotas give absolute protection (volume is capped regardless of price) while tariffs let foreign goods in if they're cheap enough. Quotas may be more effective at protecting volume share. However, quotas are more disruptive to supply chains, are politically more contentious, and don't generate government revenue. Overall quotas are STRONGER protection but more harmful [eval] — they create shortages and supply uncertainty that tariffs don't. For genuine national-security industries quotas may be justified; for ordinary economic protection, tariffs (or even better, no protection) are usually preferable. Quotas are a heavier instrument that should be used sparingly [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'protectionism'.
model_answer: Policies designed to shield domestic industries from foreign competition — including tariffs, quotas, subsidies, regulations and currency manipulation.

example_question: Identify two protectionist policies a government might use.
model_answer: Import tariffs; import quotas; subsidies to domestic producers; technical regulations favouring local goods; 'buy local' rules in government procurement; currency manipulation; export subsidies; complex customs procedures; restrictions on foreign investment.

example_question: Outline two arguments for using protectionist policies.
model_answer: Protects domestic jobs in industries vulnerable to foreign competition (especially when cheap imports come from low-wage countries), supporting national employment and economic stability. Supports 'infant industries' that need time to develop scale and expertise before competing internationally, ensuring strategic industries aren't crushed before they can compete.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two arguments against protectionism affecting {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Retaliation — when one country imposes tariffs, others usually retaliate, hurting all exporters. {BUSINESS} may benefit from protection at home but lose more abroad. Higher consumer prices — protected industries raise prices because they don't face competitive pressure, costing consumers more for everyday goods (food, clothing, electronics) and reducing real incomes that could have been spent elsewhere.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think governments should use protectionism to support businesses in their country? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Protectionism does save specific jobs and supports vulnerable industries, important for political and social stability. However, it raises consumer prices, invites retaliation, and props up uncompetitive industries that would be better served by adapting or moving to higher-value activities. Overall protectionism should be USED SPARINGLY [eval] — limited targeted use for genuinely strategic industries or temporary adjustment support can be justified, but broad protectionism harms more than it helps. Most economists agree that free trade with safety nets (retraining, regional support) outperforms protectionism. The political appeal of protectionism exceeds its economic merit [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'free trade agreements'.
model_answer: Agreements between countries to reduce or eliminate tariffs, quotas, and other barriers to trade — examples include EU, NAFTA/USMCA, ASEAN, WTO.

example_question: Identify two examples of free trade agreements or trade blocs.
model_answer: European Union (EU); USMCA (formerly NAFTA); ASEAN; Mercosur (South America); CPTPP; AfCFTA (African); UK-Australia trade agreement; WTO framework; bilateral trade agreements (UK-Japan, etc).

example_question: Outline two benefits of free trade agreements for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Easier access to partner countries' markets without tariffs, expanding the addressable market and supporting export growth. Cheaper imports of materials and components from partner countries, reducing {BUSINESS}'s production costs.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges {BUSINESS} faces in free trade environments.
model_answer: Increased competition — foreign firms can enter the home market easily, potentially with cost advantages, putting price pressure on {BUSINESS} and forcing greater efficiency or differentiation. Need for international standards — products must comply with partner countries' technical, safety and environmental rules, often requiring product modifications and certification costs.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think the UK should join more free trade agreements to help businesses? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Free trade agreements expand market access, lower input costs and force productive efficiency through competition, generally benefiting exporters and consumers. However, they expose vulnerable domestic industries to competition they may not survive, and require giving up some regulatory sovereignty. Overall the UK SHOULD pursue selective FTAs [eval] — particularly with major economies and growing markets. The economic benefits typically outweigh disruption costs, but agreements should include transition support for affected workers and industries. Pure isolation isn't viable; well-designed integration is [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'economic growth'.
model_answer: An increase in a country's GDP (total value of goods and services produced) — usually measured as the percentage change in GDP per year.

example_question: Identify two ways economic growth is measured.
model_answer: Real GDP growth rate (% change year on year); real GDP per capita; nominal GDP; gross national income; alternative measures (well-being indices, sustainability-adjusted GDP); employment rate; productivity per worker.

example_question: Outline two effects of strong economic growth on {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Higher consumer spending boosts demand for {BUSINESS}'s products, supporting sales growth. Easier access to finance — banks lend more freely and investors are more confident in growth periods, helping {BUSINESS} fund expansion.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two negative effects on {BUSINESS} of very rapid economic growth.
model_answer: Rising costs — strong demand for labour, raw materials and property pushes wages, prices and rents up faster than {BUSINESS}'s selling prices can rise, squeezing margins. Skills shortages — fast growth creates competition for skilled workers, who command higher wages and may leave for rivals, increasing {BUSINESS}'s recruitment and retention costs.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think continuous economic growth is good for businesses like {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Sustained growth lifts demand, supports investment and creates jobs that are good for most businesses. However, growth also brings rising costs (wages, materials, rent) and can damage environmental sustainability, creating long-term problems. Overall sustained MODERATE growth is good for {BUSINESS} [eval] — typical 2-3% annual real growth supports rising demand without overheating. Very fast growth (5%+) brings overheating problems; no growth means stagnant demand. Continuous growth at MODERATE rates is the sweet spot. {BUSINESS} should benefit from steady growth but plan for cyclical downturns that always eventually follow booms [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'recession'.
model_answer: A significant decline in economic activity lasting more than a few months — typically defined as two consecutive quarters of falling GDP. Characterised by falling output, rising unemployment, and reduced consumer spending.

example_question: Identify two effects of a recession on the economy.
model_answer: Falling GDP; rising unemployment; reduced consumer spending; lower business investment; falling business confidence; falling property prices; reduced government tax revenue; rising welfare costs; possible deflation.

example_question: Outline two effects of a recession on {BUSINESS}'s sales and operations.
model_answer: Demand falls as consumers spend less and delay purchases of non-essential goods, reducing {BUSINESS}'s revenue. Customers may switch to cheaper alternatives or own-brand products, eroding {BUSINESS}'s premium positioning.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two strategies {BUSINESS} could use to survive a recession.
model_answer: Cut non-essential costs — reduce discretionary spending (marketing, travel, refurbishment) to preserve cash, focusing only on essential operations and investment. Diversify customer base — if recession hits one market hard, having customers in different sectors or countries spreads risk; rapidly developing new segments can replace lost revenue from declining ones.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think recessions can ever be good for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Recessions force discipline, eliminate weak competitors, and create opportunities for strong businesses to acquire assets cheaply and gain market share. Some businesses (discount retailers, repair services) actually benefit. However, the costs (lost revenue, layoffs, customer loss) are real and damaging for most. Overall recessions can be BENEFICIAL for strong, well-prepared businesses [eval] — they can acquire weakened competitors cheaply, hire talent shed by failing rivals, and emerge stronger. For weakened or poorly-prepared businesses, recessions are damaging or fatal. The opportunity exists but depends on entering the recession with reserves and resilience [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'inflation'.
model_answer: A sustained rise in the general price level in an economy over time — usually measured by Consumer Price Index (CPI). Caused by demand exceeding supply (demand-pull) or rising costs (cost-push).

example_question: Identify two causes of inflation.
model_answer: Demand-pull (excess demand chasing limited supply); cost-push (rising input costs passed on); rising wages without productivity gains; rising commodity prices (oil); excessive money supply growth; currency depreciation; supply chain shocks; expectations of future inflation.

example_question: Outline two effects of high inflation on {BUSINESS}'s costs.
model_answer: Raw material and energy costs rise, increasing {BUSINESS}'s production costs that must be either absorbed (reducing margins) or passed on to customers (risking sales). Wage demands rise as employees seek pay rises to maintain living standards, raising payroll costs which compound over time.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could respond to high inflation.
model_answer: Raise prices in line with cost increases to protect margins — risky if customers are price-sensitive, but necessary if costs are rising faster than productivity. Improve productivity — invest in equipment or processes that increase output per worker, offsetting wage rises with greater efficiency, supporting profitability without large price rises.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think high inflation is the worst economic threat to {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: High inflation erodes margins, complicates planning, and creates uncertainty — genuine problems. However, deflation (falling prices) discourages spending and devastates economies, while deep recession destroys demand and bankrupts businesses. Overall high inflation is HARMFUL but not necessarily the worst threat [eval] — moderate inflation is normal and manageable. Severe inflation is bad; deflation or deep recession may be worse. The worst threats depend on industry — exporters face currency volatility, retailers face demand collapse, capital-intensive businesses face interest-rate shocks. Inflation is one of several economic risks {BUSINESS} must manage [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'interest rates'.
model_answer: The cost of borrowing money — usually set by central banks to control inflation and economic activity. Higher rates make borrowing more expensive and saving more attractive.

example_question: Identify two effects of higher interest rates on the economy.
model_answer: More expensive to borrow; consumer spending falls (especially on credit); business investment falls; mortgage costs rise; housing demand falls; saving increases; exchange rate may rise (attracts foreign deposits); inflation usually slows.

example_question: Outline two effects of a rise in interest rates on {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Existing variable-rate loans cost more to service, raising {BUSINESS}'s financial costs and reducing profit. New borrowing becomes more expensive, deterring investment in expansion or new equipment that requires loan finance.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways higher interest rates could affect {BUSINESS}'s customers and sales.
model_answer: Consumer borrowing falls — customers borrowing on credit cards, personal loans or mortgages face higher repayments and reduce discretionary spending, hurting demand for non-essential products. Business customers cut investment — if {BUSINESS} sells to other businesses, demand falls as customers postpone capital expenditure, particularly affecting B2B businesses with long sales cycles.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think rising interest rates always hurt {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Rising rates raise borrowing costs, reduce consumer spending and discourage investment — clear negatives for most businesses. However, cash-rich businesses benefit from higher savings rates, banks earn more on lending margins, and weaker competitors with high debt may fail, leaving market share for stronger rivals. Overall rates HURT MOST BUSINESSES [eval] in the short term, but the effect depends on debt levels, sector and competitive position. {BUSINESS} should manage debt prudently and avoid relying on cheap finance — the businesses most hurt by rising rates are those that loaded up on debt during low-rate periods. Conservative finance protects against rate cycles [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'business cycle'.
model_answer: The natural fluctuation of economic activity over time — typically through phases of growth (boom), peak, downturn (recession), trough, and recovery, then repeating.

example_question: Identify the four main phases of the business cycle.
model_answer: Recovery/growth; Boom/peak; Downturn/contraction; Recession/trough.

example_question: Outline two reasons why understanding the business cycle is useful for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Plans investment timing — invest during recoveries when costs are low and demand is rising; preserve cash before downturns. Anticipates customer behaviour — demand patterns change with the cycle (luxuries fall in recession, essentials hold steady), letting {BUSINESS} adapt product mix and marketing to current conditions.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could prepare for an economic downturn.
model_answer: Build cash reserves during good times — retain profit rather than spending it all on growth, providing buffer to survive when revenue falls and demand returns gradually. Diversify revenue streams — if heavily reliant on a few customers or one sector, downturns can be devastating; spreading across sectors and markets reduces the impact of any single area collapsing.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should expand most aggressively during economic booms? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Booms bring high demand, easy finance and confidence — natural time for expansion. However, costs (land, equipment, talent) are also highest in booms, and recessions follow leaving expanded businesses with idle capacity. Overall {BUSINESS} should NOT expand most in booms [eval] — counter-intuitively, the best time to invest is during recovery (low costs, rising demand) and even during late recession (cheap acquisitions, bargain assets). Expanding at the peak is buying high; smart businesses build cash in booms and deploy it as the cycle turns. Cycle-aware investing beats momentum-following [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'government taxation'.
model_answer: Money taken from individuals and businesses by the government — to fund public services, redistribute wealth, and influence economic behaviour. Includes income tax, corporation tax, VAT, capital gains, and excise duties.

example_question: Identify two types of tax that affect businesses.
model_answer: Corporation tax (on company profits); employer National Insurance; VAT (value added tax) on sales; business rates (property tax); capital gains tax; payroll taxes; income tax on owner's income; environmental taxes (carbon, plastic); custom duties on imports.

example_question: Outline two effects of higher business taxes on {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Lower retained profits — after-tax profit available for reinvestment or dividends falls, slowing growth funding or returns to owners. Discourages investment — when taxes take a bigger share of returns, the after-tax return on investment falls, making some projects no longer worthwhile.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways government tax changes could affect customer spending and {BUSINESS}'s sales.
model_answer: Income tax cuts increase disposable income, raising consumer spending and boosting demand for {BUSINESS}'s products. VAT changes affect prices customers pay — higher VAT reduces purchasing power and dampens demand, especially for discretionary goods, while VAT cuts can stimulate sales.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think governments should cut business taxes to support enterprise? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Lower business taxes leave more profit for investment, hiring and innovation, supporting growth and competitiveness with other countries. However, lost tax revenue must be replaced — either through higher personal taxes (cutting consumer spending) or reduced public services (cutting infrastructure, skills, healthcare that businesses also depend on). Overall the right level of business tax is COMPLEX [eval] — too high discourages investment and emigration of businesses; too low starves public services that businesses need. Most economists support moderate, competitive but not minimal business taxation. Pure tax-cut strategies often look attractive short-term but undermine the conditions for business success long-term [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'government spending'.
model_answer: Money governments spend on public services (education, healthcare, defence), infrastructure (roads, internet), welfare benefits, and economic support — funded by taxes and borrowing.

example_question: Identify two areas of government spending that affect businesses.
model_answer: Infrastructure (roads, ports, broadband); education and training (workforce skills); healthcare (employee welfare); R&D grants; defence (procurement contracts); welfare (consumer purchasing power); regulation and enforcement; legal system; transport.

example_question: Outline two ways government spending could benefit {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Infrastructure investment improves transport, energy and digital networks that {BUSINESS} relies on, reducing operating costs and supporting growth. Education spending creates a skilled workforce {BUSINESS} can recruit from, reducing training burden and supporting higher-value production.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two negative effects of high government spending on {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Higher taxes to fund the spending — businesses face higher corporation tax, business rates or VAT, reducing post-tax profits and capital available for investment. Crowding out — when government borrows heavily to fund spending, it can raise interest rates and absorb investment capital that would otherwise have flowed to private business, making finance more expensive and harder to access.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think higher government spending always benefits businesses like {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Strategic spending (infrastructure, skills, R&D) clearly benefits businesses by improving the operating environment. Welfare spending supports consumer demand. However, spending must be funded by taxes or borrowing, both of which have costs that fall partly on businesses. Overall government spending HELPS WHEN TARGETED at productive uses [eval] but harms when wasteful or excessive. Quality of spending matters more than quantity — good infrastructure investment generates better returns than poorly-designed welfare. {BUSINESS} should support efficient targeted government investment and oppose wasteful spending that just creates tax burdens without proportionate benefits [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'employment legislation (external)'.
model_answer: Laws regulating the employment relationship — covering minimum wage, working hours, equal pay, anti-discrimination, health and safety, redundancy and unfair dismissal.

example_question: Identify two areas commonly covered by employment legislation.
model_answer: Minimum wage; maximum working hours; equal pay; anti-discrimination (age, race, gender, disability); health and safety; redundancy rights; unfair dismissal; maternity/paternity leave; right to join trade unions; whistleblowing protection.

example_question: Outline two reasons why employment legislation exists.
model_answer: Protects employees from exploitation by employers, ensuring basic dignified working conditions. Creates a level playing field — when all businesses must follow the same rules, ethical employers aren't penalised by competitors who'd otherwise cut corners on pay or safety.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two impacts of employment legislation on {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Higher costs — complying with minimum wage, paid leave, pension contributions and safety requirements raises labour costs, forcing higher prices or lower profits. Reduced flexibility — strict rules on working hours, dismissal and zero-hours contracts limit how {BUSINESS} can respond to changing demand, slowing reactions when business conditions change.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think employment legislation places too heavy a burden on businesses today? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Compliance costs are real and complex regulations especially burden small businesses. However, employment laws protect workers from genuine exploitation and create stable, healthy workforces that businesses ultimately need. Overall the BURDEN IS JUSTIFIED [eval] — current employment protections are mostly basic and humane. Weakening them would lead to a race to the bottom on pay and conditions, ultimately damaging the economy by harming consumer purchasing power and worker productivity. {BUSINESS} should treat compliance as cost of doing business; pushing for weaker laws is short-sighted [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'consumer protection'.
model_answer: Laws protecting consumers from unfair business practices — including product safety, accurate description, fair pricing, returns rights, and protection from misleading advertising.

example_question: Identify two consumer protection laws or rights.
model_answer: Product safety regulations; Sale of Goods Act / Consumer Rights Act; Trade Descriptions Act; cooling-off period for distance sales; right to refund/replacement for faulty goods; truth in advertising; ban on misleading prices; data protection (GDPR); fair contract terms.

example_question: Outline two ways consumer protection laws affect {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Quality and safety standards force {BUSINESS} to maintain product quality, increasing costs but protecting customers and reputation. Required returns and refunds policies — {BUSINESS} must take back faulty goods, increasing operational costs but building customer trust.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two benefits of strict consumer protection for {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Customer trust — when customers know they're protected from fraud and faulty goods, they're more willing to buy, especially from unfamiliar businesses or online. This expands the market and supports growth. Level playing field — strict enforcement prevents unscrupulous competitors from gaining advantage by cheating customers, supporting honest businesses like {BUSINESS} who already meet high standards.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think consumer protection laws are good or bad for businesses like {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Compliance adds cost — testing, labelling, returns policies, training — squeezing margins, especially for small businesses with limited resources. However, the rules build customer trust that supports sales, prevent rivals cheating, and improve product quality across the market. Overall consumer protection BENEFITS {BUSINESS} [eval] particularly an ethical business — strong laws create a trusted marketplace that drives consumer spending. Only businesses planning to cut corners face genuine harm from consumer protection. The cost of compliance is real but the benefit of being in a regulated market is larger [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'environmental issues'.
model_answer: Concerns about the impact of business activity on the natural environment — including pollution, climate change, resource depletion, waste, and biodiversity loss.

example_question: Identify two environmental issues affecting businesses.
model_answer: Carbon emissions and climate change; plastic pollution; water pollution; deforestation; air quality; waste disposal; resource depletion; biodiversity loss; supply chain emissions; packaging waste; energy efficiency.

example_question: Outline two ways {BUSINESS} could be affected by environmental issues.
model_answer: Increasingly strict regulations — carbon taxes, plastic bans, emission limits — force {BUSINESS} to change products and processes, often at significant cost. Customer expectations — environmentally conscious customers favour sustainable brands, so failing to address environmental issues damages reputation and market share, while strong sustainability credentials become a competitive advantage.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways {BUSINESS} could reduce its environmental impact.
model_answer: Reduce energy consumption — investing in efficient equipment, lighting, insulation lowers both emissions and energy costs, with attractive payback periods plus reputational benefits. Sustainable supply chain — sourcing from suppliers with strong environmental practices and reducing transportation distance lowers the total carbon footprint, supporting marketing claims and protecting against regulation.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think environmental regulations damage {BUSINESS}'s competitiveness? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Compliance costs are real and short-term — investment in cleaner equipment, sustainable materials, certification can squeeze margins. However, regulations also stimulate innovation, reduce energy bills, meet customer demand and pre-empt future stricter rules. Overall environmental regulations SUPPORT competitiveness in the long run [eval] — businesses that resist often spend more on belated compliance than those that embrace the change. Sustainability has shifted from a cost to an opportunity for forward-looking businesses. {BUSINESS} should view environmental investment as strategic, not just compliance — early movers benefit; laggards pay later [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'sustainability'.
model_answer: Meeting current needs without compromising the ability of future generations to meet theirs — covering environmental, social, and economic sustainability dimensions.

example_question: Identify two ways {BUSINESS} could contribute to sustainable development.
model_answer: Reduce energy consumption; source sustainable materials; design products to last longer; reduce packaging waste; recycle/circular economy; pay fair wages; support local community; ethical sourcing; reduce water use; sustainable transport; carbon neutral operations.

example_question: Outline two reasons why sustainability matters to {BUSINESS}'s success.
model_answer: Customer demand — growing numbers of consumers prefer sustainable brands and will pay premium for environmentally responsible products, opening new market opportunities. Regulatory pressure — sustainability rules are tightening worldwide; {BUSINESS} that prepares now avoids the disruption and cost of catching up later under regulatory pressure.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges {BUSINESS} faces in becoming more sustainable.
model_answer: Cost — sustainable materials, renewable energy, efficient equipment often cost more upfront, squeezing margins or forcing price rises that customers may resist. Greenwashing risk — making sustainability claims that aren't fully backed by action can lead to public criticism and damage trust if exposed, requiring careful authentic communication rather than just marketing.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think focusing on sustainability is the best way for {BUSINESS} to attract customers? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Sustainability appeals to growing customer segments, builds brand loyalty and supports premium pricing — important strategic asset in many markets. However, for price-sensitive customers in commodity markets, value and quality still matter most, and pure sustainability marketing without product excellence falls flat. Overall sustainability is INCREASINGLY IMPORTANT but not universally most important [eval] — for premium consumer brands, sustainability is becoming central. For pure commodity markets, it matters less than price and reliability. {BUSINESS} should combine genuine sustainability action with strong product fundamentals — both are needed, neither alone is enough [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'renewable energy'.
model_answer: Energy from sources that are naturally replenished — solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal, biomass — as opposed to fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) which are finite and emit CO2.

example_question: Identify two examples of renewable energy sources.
model_answer: Solar (panels, solar farms); wind (onshore, offshore turbines); hydroelectric (dams); geothermal (Earth's heat); biomass (organic material); tidal/wave energy; biofuels; hydrogen (when produced from renewables).

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} might switch to renewable energy.
model_answer: Reduce long-term energy costs — although upfront investment is significant, ongoing costs of solar or wind are lower than fossil fuels, especially as electricity prices rise. Meet customer and regulatory expectations — increasing pressure from customers, investors and governments to reduce carbon emissions, so renewable energy supports brand reputation and compliance.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges {BUSINESS} might face in adopting renewable energy.
model_answer: Upfront cost — installing solar panels, switching contracts, or upgrading equipment requires significant capital investment, with payback periods of several years. Cash-constrained businesses may struggle to fund this even when the long-term economics are positive. Reliability and supply — renewable energy is intermittent (sun doesn't shine at night, wind isn't constant), so {BUSINESS} may need backup power or battery storage, adding complexity and cost to ensure reliable operations.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think investing in renewable energy is worth the cost for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Renewable energy reduces long-term costs, improves reputation, hedges against energy price volatility and meets growing regulatory and customer demands. However, upfront costs are significant and payback periods of 5-10 years may exceed the planning horizon of many businesses. Overall renewable energy IS WORTH the investment for {BUSINESS} [eval] with stable operations and reasonable scale — declining technology costs make it increasingly economic, energy costs are volatile, customer expectations rise, and regulation tightens. For very small or short-term businesses the case is weaker, but for most established firms the trend is clearly toward renewable adoption [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'ethical issues'.
model_answer: Moral concerns about business conduct — including how the business treats employees, suppliers, customers, the environment, and the community.

example_question: Identify two ethical issues that businesses commonly face.
model_answer: Fair wages and working conditions; honest marketing; ethical sourcing (no child labour, sweatshops); environmental responsibility; tax fairness (paying fair share); animal welfare; data privacy; whistleblowing; conflicts of interest; bribery and corruption.

example_question: Outline two reasons why ethical behaviour matters to {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Reputation — ethical scandals damage brand and customer trust rapidly, with long-lasting effects on sales and recruitment, while strong ethics support brand strength over time. Employee morale — workers want to work for businesses they're proud of; unethical behaviour drives away good employees and makes recruitment harder, while strong ethics attract and retain talent.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why ethical behaviour can also benefit {BUSINESS} financially.
model_answer: Customer loyalty — many customers actively seek ethical brands and remain loyal because of values alignment, supporting premium pricing and reducing churn. Risk reduction — ethical practices reduce legal, regulatory and reputational risks, lowering insurance costs and preventing the dramatic financial damage of scandals.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think being ethical is profitable for {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Ethical practice builds reputation, attracts customers, motivates staff and reduces risk — generally supporting profit. However, ethical sourcing, fair wages and environmental investment cost more, squeezing short-term margins. Overall ethics is PROFITABLE for {BUSINESS} OVER THE LONG TERM [eval] — short-term costs may rise, but ethical businesses build stronger brands, attract better talent, avoid catastrophic scandals and access growing 'conscious consumer' markets. Pure unethical short-termism eventually destroys value through scandal, regulation or talent flight. Profit and ethics are usually aligned over time; treating them as opposites is short-sighted [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'child labour'.
model_answer: The employment of children — especially in hazardous, dangerous, or exploitative conditions — that interferes with their education, health, and development. Widely considered unethical and illegal in most countries.

example_question: Identify two industries historically associated with child labour.
model_answer: Textile and garment manufacturing; cocoa farming; mining; agriculture (cotton, tobacco); brick kilns; carpet weaving; fishing; domestic service; small-scale gold mining; tobacco processing; supply chains for fast fashion.

example_question: Outline two reasons why {BUSINESS} should ensure its supply chain is free from child labour.
model_answer: Ethical responsibility — children should be in school, not working in dangerous conditions, regardless of which country they're in. Reputational and commercial risk — public exposure of child labour in supply chain triggers boycotts, lost contracts, regulatory action and lasting brand damage that costs far more than ensuring clean sourcing would.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two challenges {BUSINESS} faces in eliminating child labour from its supply chain.
model_answer: Long, complex supply chains — products often pass through many tiers of suppliers (farmer → trader → processor → manufacturer → buyer), making it hard to monitor every link. Child labour often happens at the earliest, hardest-to-see tiers. Cost of monitoring — properly auditing suppliers requires investment in inspection, certification, third-party audits and ongoing oversight, raising sourcing costs that may put {BUSINESS} at competitive disadvantage if rivals don't pay the same costs.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think responsibility for preventing child labour lies with businesses, governments, or consumers? Justify your answer.
model_answer: All three share responsibility, but in different ways. Governments must enforce laws and provide education alternatives; consumers must support ethical brands and avoid suspicious-cheap goods; businesses must audit supply chains and refuse to source from offenders. Overall responsibility lies WITH ALL THREE [eval] but businesses bear the most immediate operational responsibility since they have direct visibility and control over their supply chains. Pointing only at governments or consumers ignores that businesses choose where to buy. The most effective approach combines business accountability with government enforcement and consumer support — none alone can solve it [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'fair trade'.
model_answer: A system of trading that aims to ensure producers in developing countries receive fair prices, decent working conditions, and sustainable practices — often certified by organisations like Fairtrade Foundation.

example_question: Identify two products commonly available as fair trade certified.
model_answer: Coffee; tea; chocolate/cocoa; bananas; cotton clothing; sugar; flowers; gold; honey; wine; rice; quinoa; vanilla.

example_question: Outline two ways fair trade benefits producers in developing countries.
model_answer: Guaranteed minimum price — protects producers from price collapses that would otherwise destroy their livelihoods, providing stable income they can plan around. Premium payments funded by consumers — extra money is invested in community projects (schools, healthcare, infrastructure), improving living conditions beyond just the producer.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two reasons why {BUSINESS} might choose to sell fair trade products.
model_answer: Marketing advantage — ethical consumers actively seek fair trade products and pay premium prices, expanding {BUSINESS}'s market and supporting higher margins. Brand reputation — being associated with fair trade enhances overall brand image, supporting positioning as an ethical business and attracting employees and customers who value that.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think {BUSINESS} should sell only fair trade products in its category? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Selling only fair trade aligns commercial strategy with ethical values, attracts ethical customers and simplifies sourcing. However, fair trade products cost more, limiting sales to price-sensitive customers, and not all categories have meaningful fair trade alternatives. Overall {BUSINESS} should NOT sell ONLY fair trade [eval] in most categories — a mixed range serves both ethical and price-sensitive customers without forcing the latter to shop elsewhere. The right balance depends on category and target customer. Pure fair trade positioning works for some businesses but is unnecessarily restrictive for most [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'pressure groups'.
model_answer: Organised groups of people who try to influence businesses, governments, and public opinion to change behaviour or policy in line with their cause — examples include Greenpeace, Amnesty International, consumer associations.

example_question: Identify two well-known pressure groups.
model_answer: Greenpeace; Friends of the Earth; Amnesty International; Oxfam; PETA; WWF; ASH (anti-smoking); Which? (consumer); CND (anti-nuclear); Stonewall (LGBT rights); Extinction Rebellion.

example_question: Outline two ways pressure groups try to influence businesses.
model_answer: Public campaigns and boycotts — drawing media attention to perceived bad practices and asking consumers to stop buying, damaging the targeted business commercially. Lobbying for regulation — pushing governments to introduce laws that force all businesses including the target to change behaviour, removing the competitive disadvantage of acting alone.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two ways pressure groups can affect {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Reputational damage — even well-prepared campaigns can trigger negative news coverage and social media storms that hurt sales and recruitment for months or years. New regulatory burden — successful lobbying may lead to new laws (carbon taxes, labelling rules, sourcing restrictions) that {BUSINESS} must comply with, raising costs or restricting operations.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think pressure groups are a positive or negative force for businesses overall? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Pressure groups can damage businesses through campaigns and reputation attacks. However, they also highlight legitimate issues that society cares about, push businesses to improve practices in ways that benefit long-term reputation, and identify reforms that level the competitive playing field. Overall pressure groups are A POSITIVE FORCE on balance [eval] — they raise standards and identify ethical failings that internal compliance might miss. Smart businesses engage constructively with legitimate pressure groups, learning what stakeholders care about and adapting before being targeted. Treating them only as enemies misses the value of their feedback [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.

example_question: Define 'corporate social responsibility'.
model_answer: A business approach where companies take responsibility for their impact on society and the environment — going beyond legal requirements to act ethically, support communities, and reduce harm.

example_question: Identify two examples of corporate social responsibility activities.
model_answer: Donating to charity; volunteering programs for employees; sustainable sourcing; reducing emissions; paying living wage; supporting local community; ethical supply chain; investing in employee wellbeing; gender pay gap reporting; carbon-neutral commitments; cause marketing.

example_question: Outline two reasons why CSR matters to {BUSINESS}.
model_answer: Reputation and brand — strong CSR builds positive brand image that supports premium pricing, customer loyalty and recruitment of top talent. Risk management — businesses with strong CSR practices face less regulatory pressure, fewer scandals and lower employee turnover, all of which protect long-term performance.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Explain two benefits to {BUSINESS} of implementing CSR programmes.
model_answer: Customer attraction — many customers actively choose brands they see as responsible, willing to pay slightly more for ethical products, expanding {BUSINESS}'s market and supporting margins. Employee engagement — workers prefer to work for organisations they're proud of, raising retention, productivity and the quality of candidates {BUSINESS} can attract.
notes: Reference the business context for full marks.

example_question: Do you think CSR is genuine social value or just marketing for businesses like {BUSINESS}? Justify your answer.
model_answer: Cynical CSR (greenwashing, charity for press coverage) is real and undermines public trust. However, many businesses genuinely commit to social impact alongside profit, and even self-interested CSR delivers real benefits to communities and the environment. Overall CSR is MOSTLY MARKETING for some businesses but GENUINE for others [eval] — the difference is investment depth and accountability. Stakeholders are increasingly good at distinguishing real commitment from marketing, so insincere CSR backfires. {BUSINESS} should commit genuinely — it benefits society AND the bottom line. Cynical CSR risks reputational damage when exposed; genuine CSR is both right and profitable [eval].
notes: Reference the business context for full marks. Award up to 2 marks for evaluation/judgement.
